MINI MINUTES FOR JAN-05-2017 09:30 AM FOR DEPT 302
ORDER GRANTING MOTION OF KQED, INC. FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT OR, IN THE ALTERNATIVE, SUMMARY ADJUDICATION

LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT KQED, INC. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT OR, IN THE ALTERNATIVE, SUMMARY ADJUDICATION - DEFENDANT KQED, INC.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT IS GRANTED. AS TO THE FIRST AND THIRD CAUSES OF ACTION FOR RACE DISCRIMINATION AND RETALIATION, KQED IS ENTITLED TO JUDGMENT IN ITS FAVOR BECAUSE PLAINTIFFS LEILANI VIANA AND ELIZABETH MEDINA HAVE NOT SUFFERED AN ADVERSE EMPLOYMENT ACTION. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. (SEE MINI-MINUTES AND/OR ORDER FOR COMPLETE RULING). JUDGE: HAROLD KAHN; CLERK: CYNTHIA HERBERT; REPORTER: SHERRY SAWYER, CSR # 5976 =302/HK

MASTER CALENDAR COURT
Off Calendar Feb-01-2017 Set For Case Management Conference On May-10-17 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Of Kqed, Inc. For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Jan-05-2017 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES - ATTY. CAROL A. TREASURE & ATTY. ANN M. KARIUKI, TEL. NO. 1-414-633-8833 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. JIYUN CAMERON LEE OF THE FOLGER & LEVIN LAW FIRM, TEL. NO. 1-415-625-1075 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT KQED, INC. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication - Defendant KQED, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment is granted. As to the first and third causes of action for race discrimination and retaliation, KQED is entitled to judgment in its favor because plaintiffs Leilani Viana and Elizabeth Medina have not suffered an adverse employment action. Ms. Viana and Ms. Medina have not been demoted, had their pay reduced, or lost overtime opportunities. (UF 253-255.) KQED's refusal to provide them with the schedules of their choice is not an adverse employment action but is "merely [a decision] contrary to an employee's interest." (Malais v. Los Angeles City Fire Dep't (2007) 150 Cal.App.4th 350, 357.) Nor does the "joint hold" constitute an adverse employment action since it related to negotiations between KQED and plaintiffs' union about scheduling and therefore is analyzed in the same way as KQED's refusal to provide plaintiffs with the schedules of their choice. (UF 48-49.) KQED's refusal to grant Ms. Medina's training requests does not constitute an adverse employment action because such trainings were not necessary for Ms. Medina's job performance or for any promotional opportunities. (UF 152-153, 162, 190.) (Yanowitz v. L'Oreal, USA, Inc. (2005) 36 Cal.4th 1028, 1049.) As to the second cause of action for race-based harassment, KQED is entitled to judgment in its favor because the undisputed facts establish that Ms. Viana and Ms. Medina were not subjected to severe or pervasive comments based on race. Ms. Viana and Ms. Medina do not dispute they were never subjected to racial epithets by their supervisors. (UF 191-193, 207-209.) The evidence shows that supervisors made two comments in regards to race, which are isolated and sporadic. (Etter v. Veriflo Corp. (1998) 67 Cal.App.4th 457, 465.) Moreover, plaintiffs have not shown that Ms. Miller's yelling at Ms. Medina was motivated by racial animus. Order signed in open court. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR # 5976 =302/hk
